title: Post-9/11 GI Bill Overview
text: The Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33) provides financial support for education and housing to individuals with at least 90 days of aggregate service after September 10, 2001, or individuals discharged with a service-connected disability after 30 days. You must have received an honorable discharge. Benefits include tuition and fees paid directly to the school, a monthly housing allowance based on the zip code of your school, and a stipend for books and supplies up to $1,000 per academic year. You may transfer unused benefits to dependents if you have at least 6 years of service and agree to serve 4 more years.

title: VA Disability Compensation
text: VA disability compensation provides monthly tax-free payments to veterans who have a physical or mental condition that was caused or made worse by their military service. To qualify, you must have a current diagnosis of a physical or mental condition, evidence of an in-service event or injury that caused the condition, and a medical link connecting the current condition to your service. Ratings range from 0 to 100 percent in increments of 10. A 100 percent rating currently pays $3,737.85 per month for a veteran with no dependents. Veterans rated at 30 percent or higher may add compensation for dependents.

title: VA Home Loan Guaranty
text: The VA home loan program helps veterans, servicemembers, and surviving spouses purchase homes without a down payment in most cases. To be eligible, you must have a valid Certificate of Eligibility, meet lender credit and income standards, and the home must be for your own personal occupancy. There is a VA funding fee ranging from 1.25 percent to 3.3 percent of the loan amount. Veterans with a service-connected disability rating are exempt from the funding fee. You can apply for your Certificate of Eligibility through VA.gov, by mail, or through your lender.

title: Vocational Rehabilitation Chapter 31
text: VA Vocational Rehabilitation and Employment (VR&E, Chapter 31) helps veterans with service-connected disabilities prepare for, find, and keep suitable jobs. To be eligible, you must have a VA service-connected disability rating of at least 10 percent, or a memorandum rating of 20 percent or more, and have an employment handicap. The program provides up to 48 months of services including job training, resume development, job-seeking skills coaching, and on-the-job training. A monthly subsistence allowance is paid during training.

title: Survivors and Dependents Educational Assistance Chapter 35
text: The Survivors and Dependents Educational Assistance (DEA) program, Chapter 35, offers education and training to eligible dependents of veterans who are permanently and totally disabled due to a service-related condition or who died while on active duty or as a result of a service-related condition. Eligible beneficiaries include spouses and children between ages 18 and 26. The monthly payment rate for full-time training is $1,469 as of October 2024. Maximum entitlement is 45 months of full-time training.

title: VA Pension for Low-Income Wartime Veterans
text: VA pension is a tax-free monetary benefit payable to low-income, wartime veterans. To qualify, you must have served at least 90 days of active duty with at least 1 day during a wartime period, been discharged under conditions other than dishonorable, and meet income and net worth limits set by Congress. As of December 2023, the Maximum Annual Pension Rate for a veteran without dependents is $16,551 per year. Veterans who need help with daily activities may qualify for Aid and Attendance, which increases the benefit.

title: Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty Chapter 30
text: The Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty (MGIB-AD), Chapter 30, provides up to 36 months of education benefits to veterans and servicemembers with at least 2 years of active duty. To participate, you must have contributed $1,200 total, deducted as $100 per month for 12 months from your pay. You must also have a high school diploma or GED. The full-time monthly rate as of October 2024 is $2,324. Benefits must be used within 10 years of your last discharge from active duty.

title: CHAMPVA Health Benefits
text: CHAMPVA is a comprehensive health care benefits program in which the VA shares the cost of covered health care services with eligible beneficiaries. Eligibility requires being the spouse or child of a veteran who has been rated permanently and totally disabled for a service-connected condition, died from a VA-rated service-connected condition, or died on active duty, and not being eligible for TRICARE. There is no enrollment fee. CHAMPVA generally covers 75 percent of covered services after an annual deductible of $50 per person or $100 per family.

title: Specially Adapted Housing Grant
text: The Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) grant helps veterans with certain service-connected disabilities buy, build, or modify a home to live independently. Common qualifying disabilities include loss or loss of use of both legs, blindness in both eyes combined with loss of use of one hand, and certain severe burns. The maximum SAH grant amount for fiscal year 2024 is $109,986. Veterans may use the grant up to 3 times as long as the total does not exceed the maximum. A related benefit, the Special Home Adaptation grant, has a maximum of $22,036.

title: Dependency and Indemnity Compensation
text: Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a tax-free monetary benefit payable to surviving spouses, children, or parents of servicemembers who died while on active duty, or survivors of veterans who died from a service-related injury or disease. The surviving spouse base rate as of December 2023 is $1,562.74 per month. Additional allowances are available if the surviving spouse has dependent children, is housebound, or needs aid and attendance. A surviving spouse who remarries after age 57 may retain DIC benefits.

title: Native American Direct Loan Program
text: The Native American Direct Loan (NADL) program helps eligible Native American veterans finance the purchase, construction, or improvement of homes on Federal Trust Land. To be eligible, the veteran must be Native American or the spouse of a Native American, the tribal organization must have a Memorandum of Understanding with the VA, and the veteran must have a valid Certificate of Eligibility. The program offers a fixed interest rate, no down payment requirement in most cases, and no private mortgage insurance. The VA sets and services the loan directly.

title: Aid and Attendance and Housebound Benefits
text: Aid and Attendance and Housebound benefits are additional pension amounts paid to veterans and survivors who need help with daily activities or are confined to the home. To qualify for Aid and Attendance, a veteran must need help with activities such as bathing, dressing, or eating, be bedridden, or be in a nursing home due to a mental or physical disability. Housebound benefits apply when a veteran is substantially confined to the home due to a permanent disability. These benefits are added on top of VA pension and do not require a separate application. The additional monthly amount for Aid and Attendance as of 2024 is approximately $828.

title: VA Health Care Enrollment
text: Veterans who served in the active military, naval, or air service and were discharged under any condition other than dishonorable may apply for VA health care. VA assigns each enrolled veteran to one of eight priority groups based on disability rating, income, and other factors. Priority Group 1 includes veterans with service-connected disabilities rated 50 percent or more or determined unemployable. Veterans may apply online at VA.gov, by phone, in person at any VA medical facility, or by mail using VA Form 10-10EZ.

title: Transition Assistance Program
text: The Transition Assistance Program (TAP) helps servicemembers and their families transition from military to civilian life. Participation is mandatory for servicemembers separating after more than 180 days of continuous active duty. The core curriculum includes 5 days of workshops covering finances, resume writing, job searching, and VA benefits. Servicemembers must complete the Preseparation Counseling checklist using DD Form 2648. TAP is available at military installations worldwide and some components are available online through Joint Knowledge Online.

title: Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers
text: The Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC) supports caregivers of eligible post-9/11 veterans. To qualify, the veteran must have a serious injury incurred or aggravated in the line of duty on or after September 11, 2001, require at least 6 months of continuous personal care services, and be enrolled in VA health care. Eligible caregivers receive a monthly stipend based on the veteran's care needs, access to CHAMPVA health insurance if not otherwise covered, mental health services, caregiver training, and respite care. Applications use VA Form 10-10CG and must be submitted jointly by the veteran and caregiver.

title: VA Burial and Memorial Benefits
text: The VA provides burial and memorial benefits for eligible veterans and their family members. Eligible veterans may be buried in a national cemetery at no cost, including the grave, liner, opening and closing, and a government headstone or marker. A burial allowance up to $796 is available for service-connected deaths, and up to $300 for non-service-connected deaths for veterans receiving VA pension or compensation at time of death. Presidential Memorial Certificates are provided to next of kin. Applications for burial allowance use VA Form 21P-530.

title: Servicemembers Civil Relief Act Housing Protections
text: The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) provides housing and financial protections for active duty servicemembers. Under SCRA, servicemembers may terminate a housing lease early without penalty with proper notice if they receive deployment orders of 90 days or more or a permanent change of station. Interest rates on pre-service mortgages and other debts may be capped at 6 percent during active duty. Eviction protections apply to servicemembers and their dependents while on active duty. Benefits apply automatically upon proof of active duty status.

title: Veterans Pension Net Worth Limit
text: To qualify for VA pension, veterans must meet a net worth limit set annually by Congress. As of December 2023, the net worth limit is $155,356. Net worth includes assets plus annual income. The primary residence and vehicles are generally excluded from net worth calculations. The VA may consider asset transfers made within the 36-month lookback period before the pension application, which can affect eligibility if assets were transferred to reduce net worth.

title: VA Education Work-Study Program
text: VA-approved work-study allows students receiving VA education benefits to work part-time at VA facilities or at approved state or local government agencies in exchange for an hourly stipend. The hourly rate is at least the federal minimum wage or the applicable state minimum wage, whichever is higher. Students must be enrolled at least three-quarter time. The program is available to those using GI Bill benefits, VR&E Chapter 31, or DEA Chapter 35. Applications are submitted using VA Form 22-8691.

title: Yellow Ribbon Program
text: The Yellow Ribbon Program is an agreement between VA and private or out-of-state public schools to help cover tuition costs that exceed the Post-9/11 GI Bill maximum tuition rate for public schools. Participating schools agree to contribute a certain amount, and VA matches that contribution. Only veterans eligible for 100 percent Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits and their transferees may use Yellow Ribbon at most schools. Active duty servicemembers are not eligible. The number of Yellow Ribbon students a school accepts can vary, and availability is not guaranteed each year.
